name: ask_user_for_clarification
description: Pause execution and ask the user a single, focused question when you genuinely cannot proceed without their input. Execution is interrupted by ClarificationMiddleware via a LangGraph `Command(goto=END)`; control resumes on the user's reply, with their answer threaded back into the next turn. Use for: (1) missing information (a required path, URL, or value was not supplied); (2) ambiguous requirements (two or more equally valid interpretations); (3) approach choices (multiple valid implementations and you want user preference); (4) risk confirmation (any destructive or irreversible action — required, never assume); (5) suggestion (you have a recommendation and want approval before proceeding). Do NOT use for: questions you can answer yourself by reading code, calling `recall`, or making a reasonable judgement call. Ask one question per call; do not stack multiple unrelated questions. After this tool is called the run halts — do not emit further tool calls in the same step.
returns: Sentinel string ('Clarification request processed by middleware'); the real effect is a LangGraph interrupt that surfaces the question and any options to the client and resumes the run when the user responds. The user's reply arrives as the next message.
examples: ask_user_for_clarification(question="Which database should I migrate?", clarification_type="approach_choice", context="The repo has both a staging and a prod connection string in env.", options=[{"label": "staging", "description": "Safer first target.", "recommended": true}, {"label": "prod", "description": "Affects live users."}])

ask_user_for_clarification(question="This will delete 3,200 records in `orders`. Proceed?", clarification_type="risk_confirmation", context="Bulk cleanup requested in the user's prompt; effect is irreversible.")
ask_user_for_clarification(question="Should I update the README to reflect this rename?", clarification_type="suggestion", context="The README still references the old module name in three places.")

ask_user_for_clarification(question="Which client's invoice should I draft first?", clarification_type="approach_choice", context="Two unpaid engagements are in scope this week.", options=[{"label": "Acme Corp", "description": "Invoice due tomorrow.", "recommended": true}, {"label": "Beta Pte Ltd", "description": "Invoice due next week."}])

ask_user_for_clarification(question="Filter weekend events to family-friendly only?", clarification_type="approach_choice", context="You asked for Singapore events this weekend; the source has 40+ entries.", options=[{"label": "Family-friendly only", "recommended": true}, {"label": "All events", "description": "Includes nightlife and 18+."}])

question.description: The clarification question. Be specific, self-contained, and answerable in one short reply. One question per call. End with a question mark.

clarification_type.description: Why clarification is needed. Use `risk_confirmation` for any destructive action (file deletion, prod writes, force-push, dropping data) — never skip the confirmation step. Use `approach_choice` or `suggestion` when `options` are supplied.

context.description: Optional one-sentence context so the user understands why you're asking (e.g. what you tried, what's ambiguous, what's at stake). Omit if the question is fully self-explanatory.

options.description: Optional structured choices for `approach_choice` or `suggestion`. The UI renders each as a clickable option with `label` as the title and `description` as helper text. Provide at most one entry with `recommended: true` — the UI highlights it.

label.description: Short option title (1-5 words). Displayed prominently.

description.description: Optional one-line helper text explaining the option, its trade-offs, or its implications.

recommended.description: If true, the UI marks this option as the suggested choice. At most one option per call should set this to true.

name: present_files
description: Surface one or more workspace files to the user so they can view, download, or interact with them in the client UI. Call this AFTER writing artifacts the user is meant to consume — reports, generated documents, charts, code outputs, etc. Only paths under `/mnt/user-data/workspace` may be presented; paths outside that root are rejected with an error. Supported renderable types include PDF, MD, CSV, XLSX, common code/text formats, and images (PNG, JPG, SVG). Do NOT use for files you only need to read for your own processing — that's what `read_file` is for. Safe to call in parallel with other tools; an artifact reducer merges concurrent updates without conflict.
returns: Command that appends the normalized virtual paths to `state.artifacts` (deduplicated by the merge reducer) and emits a tool message with the presented paths. The frontend then renders the files in the artifact panel.
examples: present_files(filepaths=["/mnt/user-data/workspace/report.md"])
present_files(filepaths=["/mnt/user-data/workspace/invoice-2026-Q2-acme.xlsx", "/mnt/user-data/workspace/cover-letter.pdf"])
present_files(filepaths=["/mnt/user-data/workspace/case-summary.md", "/mnt/user-data/workspace/exhibits-index.pdf", "/mnt/user-data/workspace/timeline-chart.png"])

filepaths.description: Absolute paths under `/mnt/user-data/workspace`. Paths outside that root return an error. Group related artifacts in a single call rather than calling once per file. Recommended max ~10 paths per call.

name: recall
description: Search long-term memory for facts and context relevant to a short query. Searches both the workspace scope (per-thread memories) and the global scope (cross-thread / org-wide memories) when both are enabled. Call this BEFORE assuming you have to ask the user for context that may already exist — e.g. the user's role, prior decisions, project or case conventions, regular clients/vendors, dietary or scheduling preferences, preferred tools. Short factual phrases (`"preferred invoice format"`, `"dietary restrictions"`, `"deployment workflow"`) outperform full-sentence questions because the underlying retriever is keyword + embedding based, not conversational. Use cases span software work, legal / administrative case management, and lifestyle lookups. Returns the top-K matches above a relevance threshold; if nothing relevant is stored, you get an explicit 'No relevant memory found.' message and should fall back to asking the user or using sensible defaults.
returns: JSON string `{query, results: [{id, scope, content, category, confidence, score, source}]}` where `score` is the relevance score (higher = more relevant) and `scope` is `workspace` or `global`. Returns the literal string 'No relevant memory found.' when no results clear the threshold, and 'Memory scopes are disabled.' when the memory subsystem is turned off in config.
examples: recall(query="preferred invoice format")
recall(query="client X case notes")
recall(query="dietary restrictions")
recall(query="regular suppliers")
recall(query="preferred python formatter")

query.description: Short factual phrase (2-6 words works best). Avoid full sentences and question words — `"linter preference"` retrieves better than `"What linter does the user prefer?"`.

name: setup_agent
description: Create or replace a custom CapyHome agent by writing its SOUL.md personality file and a short description. The agent name is taken from runtime context (set by the platform when the user opens the agent editor) — it is NOT a parameter, so do not include it in the call. Calling this again for the same agent fully overwrites the existing SOUL.md and config.yaml. Use when the user explicitly asks to set up, configure, or update a custom agent's personality. Do NOT call speculatively or as part of normal work — this is a configuration tool, not a runtime behavior switch.
returns: Command that writes SOUL.md (and config.yaml for non-default agents) to the agent's directory and emits a tool message naming the agent. On any error during file writing the directory is rolled back and an error message is returned instead.
examples:
setup_agent(soul="# Personality
Focused researcher.

# Goals
Answer with sources.

# Tool restrictions
No destructive commands.

# Style
Terse, citation-first.", description="Source-cited research helper")

setup_agent(soul="# Personality
Meticulous legal assistant familiar with Singapore civil procedure.

# Goals
Help the user manage case files, extract obligations from contracts, and draft client-facing summaries.

# Tool restrictions
Never edit a contract draft without explicit risk_confirmation.

# Style
Formal, neutral, jurisdiction-aware.", description="Legal case management assistant")

setup_agent(soul="# Personality
Warm, organised admin partner.

# Goals
Generate invoices, expense reports, and client correspondence in XLSX/MD.

# Tool restrictions
Always present generated files before considering work done.

# Style
Clear, numbers-first, no jargon.", description="Admin / invoicing helper")

soul.description: Full SOUL.md content defining the agent's personality and behavior. Recommended section headers: `# Personality`, `# Goals`, `# Tool restrictions`, `# Style`. Markdown is preserved verbatim; do not wrap in code fences.

description.description: One-line description of what the agent does (under ~100 characters). Shown in the agent picker as the agent's subtitle.

name: task
description: Delegate one focused objective to a specialized subagent that runs in its own isolated context window. Use to preserve your own context for verbose exploration, run parallel independent research, or isolate noisy command execution. Objectives span software engineering, legal / administrative case work, document generation, and lifestyle lookups (events, dining, shopping) — pick the `subagent_type` that matches the work, not the surface domain. The subagent does NOT see this conversation — its `prompt` must be fully self-contained: include the objective, any constraints, what to return, and the format you want. Subagent responses above ~20,000 tokens are truncated; structure the delegation so the answer is bounded (ask for a summary, a top-N list, or a citation block, not a full dump). Do NOT use for: simple single-step work you can do directly; tasks that need mid-run user clarification (subagents cannot interrupt for input); work that requires reading or modifying this conversation's state. Subagents can themselves call most tools but cannot call `task` (no recursive delegation).
returns: String containing the subagent's final response (truncated if it exceeds ~20k tokens). Returns an error string if `subagent_type` is unknown, if called while a plan is still in `draft`, or if the subagent times out. Lifecycle events (`task_started`, `task_running`, `task_completed`, `task_failed`, `task_timed_out`) stream out-of-band to the client.
examples: task(description="Research async patterns", prompt="Survey Python asyncio cancellation patterns in 2024-2025 sources. Return 3-5 sources with one-sentence summaries and direct URL citations. Focus on structured concurrency primitives (TaskGroup, anyio).", subagent_type="source-researcher")

task(description="Find weekend events", prompt="Find 5-8 family-friendly events happening in Singapore between 2026-05-30 and 2026-06-01. For each event return: name, venue, time, one-line description, ticket/price info if any, and the source URL. Skip nightlife and 18+ events.", subagent_type="source-researcher")

task(description="Organise case files", prompt="Enumerate the files under /mnt/user-data/uploads/case-acme-v-beta/. Group them by document type (pleadings, evidence, correspondence, court orders, expert reports). Return a markdown index: per group, list filename and a one-line role description. Do not open or modify the files.", subagent_type="general-purpose")

task(description="Run full test suite", prompt="Run `make test` from the repo root and report: (1) pass/fail count, (2) the names and first 5 lines of any failing test, (3) total duration. Do not attempt to fix failures.", subagent_type="bash")

description.description: Short (3-5 word) label for UI and log display, e.g. `"Research async patterns"`. Shown verbatim in the activity timeline; keep it task-focused, not verb-only.

prompt.description: The full, self-contained objective for the subagent. Must include: (1) what to accomplish, (2) any constraints or scope limits, (3) the exact return format (e.g. `"return 3-5 sources with citations"`). The subagent does not see this conversation — explain context it needs.

subagent_type.description: Which subagent to invoke. `general-purpose`: all-tool exploratory worker, default max_turns from config. `bash`: shell-command specialist for verbose output. `source-researcher`: one narrow external/live-source objective; default max_turns=25; auto-narrows broad prompts. `docs-explorer`: searches the local corpus / mounted documents in `/mnt/user-data/workspace/.docs`. `comparison-dimension-researcher`: researches one comparison dimension across a fixed option set; default max_turns=25. `synthesis-reviewer`: reviews collected findings for coverage, contradictions, citations, and freshness. `vault-source-researcher`: autoresearch helper that writes findings into the knowledge vault.

max_turns.description: Optional override for the subagent's recursion limit (LangGraph `recursion_limit`). Defaults to the subagent's configured max (25 for researchers, higher for general-purpose). Lower to bound cost on simple tasks; raise only if the default is provably too small.

name: view_image
description: Load an image file into the model's vision channel so it can be analysed visually. Use when you need to SEE image contents yourself — interpret a screenshot, read text from a chart, identify objects, etc. This tool requires a vision-capable model and is only exposed when one is in use. Pair with `present_files` when the user should ALSO see the image — `view_image` makes it visible to you, `present_files` makes it visible to them. Do NOT use for non-image files (use `present_files` or `read_file`) or to bulk-display many images (use `present_files` instead). Supported formats: `.jpg`, `.jpeg`, `.png`, `.webp`. Keep images under ~10 MB to avoid token-budget blow-ups.
returns: Command that appends the base64 payload and mime type to `state.viewed_images`, then emits a confirmation tool message. The image is injected into the next model call as a vision input. Returns an error message string if the path is invalid, missing, not absolute, or has an unsupported extension.
examples: view_image(image_path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/screenshot.png")
view_image(image_path="/mnt/user-data/uploads/diagram.jpg")
view_image(image_path="/mnt/user-data/uploads/receipt-2026-05-22.jpg")
view_image(image_path="/mnt/user-data/uploads/event-poster.png")

image_path.description: Absolute path to the image. Workspace virtual paths (`/mnt/user-data/workspace/...`, `/mnt/user-data/uploads/...`) are resolved to the thread-scoped physical path. Supported extensions: `.jpg`, `.jpeg`, `.png`, `.webp`. Keep file size under ~10 MB.

name: write_todos
description: Create or update the structured todo list / DAG that tracks multi-step work. Items progress `pending` → `in_progress` → `completed` (or `blocked`). Items can declare dependencies via `depends_on`; downstream items remain `blocked` until their dependencies complete. Existing items are patched by `id`; items with a new or omitted `id` are appended as new nodes. Use proactively whenever a task has 3+ distinct steps, when the user lists multiple things, or when work involves dependent stages. Mark each todo `completed` immediately after finishing it — do not batch completions. In Plan Mode while `plan.status='draft'`, `completed` status is NOT allowed and all ids must use the `todo-N` prefix — finalise progression after plan approval. Once `plan.status='completed'` the graph is frozen and further mutations are rejected.
returns: Command that writes `state.todo_graph` (`{nodes, ready_ids, updated_at}`), refreshes the legacy `state.todos` list for UI compatibility, and syncs the plan handoff files. On rejection emits a tool message tagged with one of: `draft_completion_blocked`, `completed_plan_frozen`, `validation_failed`.
examples: write_todos(todos=[{"id": "todo-1", "content": "Outline doc structure", "status": "in_progress"}])
write_todos(todos=[{"id": "todo-1", "status": "completed"}, {"id": "todo-2", "content": "Email invoice to client for approval", "status": "in_progress", "depends_on": ["todo-1"]}])

write_todos(todos=[{"id": "todo-1", "content": "Draft Q2 invoice for Acme Corp", "status": "in_progress"}, {"id": "todo-2", "content": "Generate expense summary XLSX", "status": "pending"}, {"id": "todo-3", "content": "Email both files to client", "status": "pending", "depends_on": ["todo-1", "todo-2"]}])
write_todos(todos=[{"content": "Survey weekend events in Singapore", "status": "pending", "owner": "subagent", "subagent_type": "source-researcher"}])

todos.description: List of todo updates. Reuse an existing `id` to patch an existing node; omit or use a new `id` to create a new node. Only the fields you supply are written — unspecified fields preserve their prior values on patches.

id.description: Optional stable identifier. Reuse to patch an existing todo, omit (or supply a new value) to create one. In draft plans the id must match `todo-N`.

content.description: One-line description of the work. Phrase as an imperative action (`"Add login form"`, not `"login form"`).

status.description: Lifecycle state. Use `in_progress` for exactly one todo at a time (the one you are actively working on). `blocked` is reserved for waiting on a dependency. `completed` is forbidden while plan.status='draft'.

depends_on.description: Ids of todos that must reach `completed` before this todo can transition out of `blocked` into `pending`/`in_progress`. The graph must remain acyclic — cycles cause the whole update to be rejected.

owner.description: Who executes this todo. Default `lead`. Use `subagent` to mark work that should be delegated via `task`; set `subagent_type` alongside.

subagent_type.description: Required when `owner=subagent`. Names the subagent that will execute this todo.

target_endpoint.description: Routing hint for the model endpoint. `primary` for the main reasoning model, `helper` for cheaper triage. Defaults to `primary` for lead-owned work.

tool_budget.description: Optional soft cap on tool calls this todo is expected to need. Used by middlewares for progress / stall detection.

name: bash
description: Execute a bash command inside the per-thread sandbox. Use for command-line tasks that the dedicated tools cannot cover: running scripts, git operations, package installs, file format conversions (PDF → markdown, image resize/crop), XLSX/CSV generation via python, network probes, build/test commands. Use `python` to run Python code and `pip install` to install packages. PREFER the dedicated tools where they apply — they are faster and safer: `read_file` over `cat`/`head`/`tail`, `write_file` over `echo >`/heredocs, `str_replace` over `sed`, `ls` over `ls -la`. Always use absolute paths; virtual `/mnt/user-data/...` paths are automatically translated to the thread-scoped physical path. The sandbox is stateful across calls within a thread (working directory persists, files persist) but shell variables and `cd` between calls do NOT persist — `cd` inside a single command, or prefix with an absolute path. Commands run synchronously; this tool does not support backgrounded long-running processes — for those, write a script that detaches itself or use the appropriate sandbox/community tool.
returns: String containing the command's combined stdout+stderr (sandbox provider decides interleaving). Returns `Error: ...` on sandbox runtime failure, permission error, or unexpected exception. Exit codes are not surfaced separately — check the output text for error markers.
examples: bash(description="List repo root", command="ls -la /mnt/user-data/workspace")
bash(description="Convert contract PDF to markdown", command="markitdown /mnt/user-data/uploads/contract-acme-v3.pdf > /mnt/user-data/workspace/contract-acme-v3.md")

bash(description="Generate invoice XLSX from CSV", command="python -c 'import pandas as pd; pd.read_csv(\"/mnt/user-data/workspace/invoice.csv\").to_excel(\"/mnt/user-data/workspace/invoice.xlsx\", index=False)'")
bash(description="Run unit tests", command="cd /mnt/user-data/workspace && pytest tests/ -v")
bash(description="Install package", command="pip install pandas openpyxl")

description.description: Short one-line explanation of why you're running this command. Shown in the activity timeline. Pass FIRST in the call (positional convention).

command.description: The bash command to execute. Use absolute paths for files and directories. Chain dependent steps with `&&`; do not assume shell state persists between separate `bash` calls. Do not include interactive prompts — pass non-interactive flags (`-y`, `--yes`, `--non-interactive`).

name: ls
description: List the contents of a directory in tree format, up to 2 levels deep. Faster and more readable than `bash("ls -la ...")` for quick discovery — prefer this when you just want to see what's in a directory. Returns each child on its own line with simple indentation for the second level. Does NOT honor `.gitignore` — hidden and ignored files appear in the listing. For deeper recursion, exact-name file search, or filtering, use `bash` with `find`/`tree`/`fd`. Empty directories return the literal string `(empty)`.
returns: Newline-joined tree of children (max 2 levels). Returns the literal `(empty)` for empty directories, `Error: Directory not found: <path>` if the path does not exist, `Error: Permission denied: <path>` if unreadable, or `Error: ...` on other sandbox failures.
examples: ls(description="Inspect uploads", path="/mnt/user-data/uploads")
ls(description="Survey repo root", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace")

description.description: Short one-line explanation of why you're listing this directory. Shown in the activity timeline. Pass FIRST in the call.

path.description: Absolute path to the directory to list. Virtual `/mnt/user-data/...` paths are translated automatically.

name: read_file
description: Read the contents of a text file: source code, configuration, logs, markdown, etc. Supply `start_line` and `end_line` TOGETHER (both or neither) to read a 1-indexed inclusive range; omit both to read the whole file. Prefer reading a specific range for large files — full reads are wasteful for files over a few hundred lines. If you need to inspect a file you just edited, do NOT re-read it to verify the edit landed: `write_file` and `str_replace` return `OK` only on success, so a successful tool result already proves the change was applied. Do NOT use this tool for binary files or images — use `view_image` for images, and a more specific tool (or `bash(file ...)`) for unknown binary content.
returns: String containing the file contents (or the requested line slice). Returns the literal `(empty)` for empty files, `Error: File not found: <path>` if missing, `Error: Path is a directory, not a file: <path>` if you point at a directory, `Error: Permission denied reading file: <path>` if unreadable, or `Error: ...` on other failures.
examples: read_file(description="Check entrypoint", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/main.py")
read_file(description="Review contract obligations", path="/mnt/user-data/uploads/contract-acme-v3.md")
read_file(description="Inspect logs around the crash", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/run.log", start_line=100, end_line=160)
read_file(description="Read invoice line items", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/invoice-2026-Q2-acme.csv")

description.description: Short one-line explanation of why you're reading this file. Shown in the activity timeline. Pass FIRST in the call.

path.description: Absolute path to the file to read. Virtual `/mnt/user-data/...` paths are translated automatically. Text files only — use `view_image` for images.

start_line.description: Optional 1-indexed inclusive start line. Must be supplied TOGETHER with `end_line`. If only one of the pair is provided, the range is ignored and the whole file is returned.

end_line.description: Optional 1-indexed inclusive end line. Must be supplied TOGETHER with `start_line`. If `end_line` exceeds the file length, you get the lines up to the actual end with no error.

name: write_file
description: Write or append text content to a file. By default this OVERWRITES the existing file at `path` — set `append=true` to add to the end without clobbering. Creates parent directories as needed; you do not need to mkdir first. Use this for creating new files or doing full rewrites; for targeted edits to an existing file, prefer `str_replace` so you only send the diff. Do NOT use to write binary content — only UTF-8 text is supported. After a successful call you can trust the file is on disk; do not re-read it to verify.
returns: The literal string `OK` on success. Returns `Error: Permission denied writing to file: <path>` if unwritable, `Error: Path is a directory, not a file: <path>` if you target a directory, `Error: Failed to write file '<path>': <reason>` on OS-level failure, or `Error: ...` on other failures.
examples:
write_file(description="Generate invoice CSV", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/invoice-2026-Q2-acme.csv", content="Item,Quantity,Unit Price,Total
Consulting hours,40,250,10000
Reimbursable expenses,1,1250,1250
")

write_file(description="Save case summary", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/case-acme-v-beta-summary.md", content="# Case summary

## Parties
- Plaintiff: ...
- Defendant: ...

## Key dates
...
")
write_file(description="Append log entry", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/run.log", content="\n[done] step 3 complete\n", append=true)
write_file(description="Save research report", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/report.md", content="# Findings\n...")

description.description: Short one-line explanation of why you're writing this file. Shown in the activity timeline. Pass FIRST in the call.

path.description: Absolute path to the file. Parent directories are created automatically. Virtual `/mnt/user-data/...` paths are translated.

content.description: Text content to write. UTF-8 only. To create an empty file pass an empty string.

append.description: If true, append to the file instead of overwriting. Defaults to false (overwrite). Append mode still creates the file if it doesn't exist.

name: str_replace
description: Replace a substring inside an existing file. By default `old_str` must appear EXACTLY ONCE in the file — supply enough surrounding context (typically 3-5 lines before and after the change point, including whitespace and indentation EXACTLY as in the file) to make the match unique. Set `replace_all=true` to replace every occurrence, e.g. for renames. Prefer this over `write_file` for targeted edits: it is precise, minimises the payload, and fails loudly if your assumption about the file's contents is wrong. If `old_str` is not found, OR appears more than once when `replace_all=false`, the call fails without modifying the file — fix the context and retry, do not silently switch to `write_file`.
returns: The literal string `OK` on success. Returns `Error: String to replace not found in file: <path>` if `old_str` is absent, `Error: File not found: <path>` if the file is missing, `Error: Permission denied accessing file: <path>` if unreadable/unwritable, or `Error: ...` on other failures. If the file is empty, returns `OK` without writing.
examples: str_replace(description="Fill client name in contract draft", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/contract-draft.md", old_str="PLACEHOLDER_CLIENT", new_str="Acme Corp Pte Ltd", replace_all=true)
str_replace(description="Update invoice due date", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/invoice-2026-Q2-acme.csv", old_str="Due,2026-06-30", new_str="Due,2026-07-15")
str_replace(description="Bump version", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/pyproject.toml", old_str="version = \"0.1.0\"", new_str="version = \"0.2.0\"")
str_replace(description="Rename function across file", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/utils.py", old_str="def old_name(", new_str="def new_name(", replace_all=true)

description.description: Short one-line explanation of why you're replacing the substring. Shown in the activity timeline. Pass FIRST in the call.

path.description: Absolute path to the file. Virtual `/mnt/user-data/...` paths are translated automatically.

old_str.description: Substring to replace. MUST be unique in the file unless `replace_all=true`. Include 3-5 lines of context before/after the change point with EXACT whitespace and indentation as in the file. Newlines are written as `\n` in JSON.

new_str.description: Replacement substring. Must be different from `old_str`. Preserve the indentation of `old_str` if the replacement spans multiple lines.

replace_all.description: If true, replace every occurrence of `old_str` (use for renames, bulk substitutions). Defaults to false (single occurrence; uniqueness enforced).
